compiled_state.title: Source attribution: track which skill and agent produced each code change
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
Add provenance tracking so every code change can be attributed to the skill, agent, and task that produced it.

## Reference: antigravity-awesome-skills Source Attribution

antigravity-awesome-skills (30k stars) maintains a source attribution ledger that tracks the origin of every skill: self (maintainer-authored), community (contributor PR), or upstream repo. This provenance tracking enables accountability across 1,340+ skills from many contributors.

DSO should apply the same principle at the code change level — not just tracking which skill exists, but which skill/agent/task produced each line of code in the host project.

## Problem

When a bug is discovered in code written by a sub-agent, there is no fast way to determine:
- Which skill orchestrated the work (sprint, fix-bug, debug-everything)
- Which sub-agent wrote the code (model tier, agent type)
- Which task ticket the code was implementing
- What checkpoint state the agent was in when it wrote the code
- Whether the code passed review, and what the review scores were

This information exists in git history and ticket comments but is scattered and expensive to reconstruct. A bug in a function requires searching git blame → commit message → ticket ID → ticket comments → checkpoint notes → review findings — across multiple systems.

## What to Track

For each commit produced by DSO workflows:

### In the Commit Message (Structured Trailer)
\`\`\`
feat(auth): add login endpoint

Implements POST /api/auth/login with JWT token generation.

DSO-Skill: sprint
DSO-Agent: dso:code-reviewer-standard
DSO-Task: task-abc-1234
DSO-Story: story-def-5678
DSO-Epic: epic-ghi-9012
DSO-Model: sonnet
DSO-Review-Score: 4.2/5
\`\`\`

### In the Telemetry Log (Detailed)
- Full agent dispatch context (prompt size, model, duration)
- Checkpoint progression (which checkpoints completed)
- Review findings summary (dimensions, scores, resolution attempts)
- Files read before editing (read_first compliance)
- Token usage for this task

## Integration Points

1. **Commit workflow** — automatically append DSO trailers to commit messages based on the active task/story/epic context
2. **Git blame enrichment** — a /dso:blame command that reads git blame + DSO trailers to show skill/agent/task attribution per line
3. **Bug investigation** — fix-bug should query DSO trailers on the blamed commit to understand the context in which the buggy code was written (which model, which review score, which skill)
4. **Retro analytics** — aggregate attribution data to identify which skills/agents/models produce the most bugs, highest review scores, or most rework
5. **Review context** — when reviewing code, the reviewer agent can see what produced the code under review (was it a haiku sub-agent? an opus architectural decision?)

## Design Considerations

- Trailers are git-native (recognized by git interpret-trailers) and don't pollute the human-readable commit message
- Keep trailers lightweight — IDs and scores only, not full descriptions
- Telemetry log has the detailed data; trailers are the index
- Must not break existing commit message conventions or Conventional Commits format
- Attribution should be opt-in via dso-config.conf (attribution.enabled=true) for projects that want it

comments.body: MVP slice extracted as epic 170b-2ac9 (Commit-level source attribution via git-native trailers). Covers commit trailers only. This parent epic retains: /dso:blame command, bug investigation integration, retro analytics, review context enrichment.
comments.author: Claude
